moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the Van Geet Opening, an unconventional first move that prepares a quick Nc3 while quietly controlling b4 and supporting potential queenside expansion. This move looks modest, but it avoids the standard 1.e4 or 1.d4 openings, which can catch opponents off-guard and lead to positions where your strategic understanding matters more than memorized theory. The idea is to develop flexibly and seize control of the center once Black commits their pieces.

Black claims the center immediately with this classical response, as is typical in the Battambang Variation. By playing e5, Black establishes a pawn on an excellent central square and opens lines for the bishop on f8 and queen on d8. This central foothold gives Black the space to develop comfortably, though we still have flexibility in how we respond without being locked into theoretical continuations.

Now we complete our opening setup by placing the knight on its natural square, controlling d5 and e4 while preparing to support further central play or kingside operations. The Nc3 develops with purpose—it contests the e4 and d5 squares, limits Black's pawn breaks, and keeps multiple options open depending on how Black develops. From c3, the knight can reroute to d5 (if Black allows), e2, or f3 depending on the position's needs.
